posts.cooked:
Thanks for the corrections Jonathan and John. My french is quite rusty and I don’t speak any italian.

Chris: I always pronounce it key vet.

Printemps is kind of like a combination of prahn and prin without emphasizing the “n” and the temps is said with a very soft “p” that almost fades away making it sound like “tom”. But the vowels are made in the back of the throat, so there’s no real English equivalent.

And their “r” is not like a midwestern American “r” either - that’s a uniquely American sound that other people have trouble with.

For the “in”, you can try saying “sang” but stop just before you get to the “ng” part and make it more nasal.

For Bourgueil, you can start by saying “boo” like a ghost and then adding the “r” that you swallow, almost making a gargling sound, and then the “geuil” sounds a little like “goy” but it actually has a rising inflection on the end.

Unlike Italian and Spanish, French is pronounced more in the back of the throat and it seems like they’re swallowing their words. The other two are much brighter, coming from the front of the mouth, and that’s why there are so many great opera singers who are Spanish or Italian - they’re almost made to sing. And easier languages for Americans.

But you can get your own pronunciation guides - go to a site like Forvo, or howtopronounce, or something like that and you can get an audio example. Keep in mind that people from different areas have different accents though!

The u sound in French can be produced by pursing our lips as if to whistle (you know how to whistle, Steve, don"t you? You just put your lips together and blow) and then trying to say e. posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.cooked:
A couple of thoughts…

First, I am a stickler for proper pronunciation in most cases. There are some things, however, that just sound so pretentious and affected when said correctly by an English speaker during the course of a conversation in English, that, despite being technically correct, should be avoided, I think.

Second, the question of where the accent falls in multi-syllabic words can be a tricky one, since not all languages are as heavily stressed as others. English is particularly insistent about the stressed syllable(s), so we English speakers want to insist words in other languages should behave the same. It’s hard for us to get our heads out of the music, if you will, of our own language and into the music of another - in some ways even more difficult than making the French “u” sound or the guttural “r” or the German “ch.”

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
Todd Tucker:

Vajra is pronounced “vi ruh”

with the i being a long i, rhyming with “pie”, accent on the first syllable.

That’s right. It’s like Gaja (guy-yah). In Piemontese dialect, the J after an A is like an I. Other examples are the vineyard Rabaja (rah-by-yah) and egg-heavy Piemontese fettucini, tajarin (tie-ya-rin). In these, I think the I forms a dipthong with the A that’s close to tie or guy in English.

I can’t think of an example of a J after any other vowel, but I’m no esperto di dialetto. Oliver McCrum knows a fair deal of Piemontese, so he would be the one to ask for more examples.

Jonathan has it right.

Dennis - The G is not sounded. (Hopefully you don’t pronounce a hard G in Champagne.) Instead it alters the consonant after it. Something similar happens in Italian with a G before an N, so there is not audible G in tagliarini – it’s pronounced tah-lyi-a-ri-ni. (If you’ve been saying tag-lee-ah-ri-ni your whole life, I hope this won’t come as too much of a shock. You have lots of company.)

Other consonants in French are also altered by what preceeds them: E.g., vieille, which is (VERY roughly) pronounced sort of vee-ay-yah.

GregT:

Guillaume - thank you for pointing out that the same sound can come from different letters. My mother was my first French teacher and that’s one thing that I never got until much later in my life - you just learned how certain words were said and that was that. For an American, it’s one thing that makes French so difficult.

The good thing is that it’s pretty much the same in English, it can be downright impossible to guess how to pronounce a word just based on its spelling, so we’re equals in this sense!

Chris Seiber:
Sarah Kirschbaum:

Second, the question of where the accent falls in multi-syllabic words can be a tricky one, since not all languages are as heavily stressed as others.

There really isn’t accenting in multisyllabic French words, as far as I understand. That is one of the important keys to learning how to pronounce French words.

This is very important and leads to a lot of misunderstandings. Put the stress where it doesn’t belong and suddenly natives won’t understand the word any more. BUT in French there’s basically no stress, at least not at the syllable level but rather at the word or even sentence level.

See e.g. the Wikipedia article :

However, some languages, such as French and Mandarin, are sometimes analyzed as lacking lexical stress entirely.



French words are sometimes said to be stressed on the final syllable, but this can be attributed to the prosodic stress that is placed on the final syllable (or, if that is a schwa, the next-to-final syllable) of any string of words in that language, and hence also on the last syllable of a word analyzed in isolation.

The schwa happens e.g. in Morey-Saint-Denis, where the ‘e’ in the last word is very rarely pronounced, so it sounds like “dnee”.

But I can understand that it’s tough when your native language makes ample use of stress (English, Italian…) to try to speak in an apparent monotone.

Another issue is that most vowels in French are a lot shorter than in English, so it often sounds like Sham-BOWWWWWWWL Moose-y-GNEEEEEEE to our ears because of the extra, unwelcome stress on vowels. It’s usually a good idea to try to clip the vowels a bit.

Warren Taranow:

“Briailles” as in Chandon de Briailles

Very roughly bree-EYE-(yuh), with only a modest stress on the middle syllable and almost no vowel after the Y sound.

In French, LL following an I which, in turn, is preceded by another vowel, is pronounced like a subtle Y and there is no L sound. Thus “vieille” (old) is roughly vee-EH-(yuh), no L sound.

By contrast, “mille” (thousand), with its single middle vowel, is roughly meal.

IlkkaL:

Do people here find that there is something wrong with the pronunciations on Forvo? Seems like trying to answer the queries here in typing is just a whole lot of work and creates very “Americanized” versions as “é” does not really sound the same as “ay”.

It’s not so easy to represent “é” in non-technical notation for English speakers. This UK reference site on French pronunciation doesn’t even attempt to give an equivalent, for instance, although it does for “è.”

This guide says E with an acute accent “denotes the pronunciation /e/ (as “e” in “hey”; somewhere between “e” in “bet” and “ee” in “see”).”

“Hey” is probably as close as we’ll get. (FYI, I suspect that a linguist would tell you that “hey” and “hay” are pronounced differently, though I doubt most lay people would detect that.)

This is why the International Phonetic Alphabet was invented in the 1800s, when modern linguistics really got going, as a universal system for recording sounds.

What makes French pronunciation difficult for English speakers (whether British, American or other), and vice versa, is that (a) both languages have a very large set of vowel sounds and dipthongs (combined vowels) (b) both languages have multiple ways of spelling the same vowel sounds and (c) there are a lot of vowels sounds in each that do not overlap. Spanish and Italian have much small vowel sets, for instance. (This is not to mention the insanely irregular spelling of vowel sounds in English.)

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
RichardFlack:

Or consonants for that matter. I suspect they are bigger issue for non native speakers.
f=gh=ph etc.

Yes, the consonants aren’t consistent in value, but it’s not as bad as the vowels.

F is pretty much unambiguous in English, and PH, which occurs I think only in words of Greek origin, always =F, and PH is also found in other European languages that absorbed things from Greek. Speakers of other European languages won’t have any doubt how to pronounce “Philip” or “philosophy.” GH=F is the only really weird one, and that’s quite uncommon (“tough,” “rough”).

By contrast, the sounds of A, E, I, O, U, Y, AE, AI, AU, EI, EU, IE, IO, IU, OA, OI, OU and U are indeterminate/ambiguous out of context. And their value varies widely in different regions in the English-speaking world. (I had a car rental clerk in Adelaide, Australian record my drivers licence as being from the USI. )

A linguist here, maybe I can be of some help.

Warren Taranow:

Hudelot-Noëllat?

The double-L is pronounced the “Spanish way” after “i” in French, at other times as actual "L"s

I wish John could’ve explained the umlauted German u (ü), because that’s the first syllable in “Hudelot”; I’d pronounce it “ü-de-loh”. For people who really don’t know how to pronounce it, I guess it’s best to google some videos on how to pronounce “ü”, because there really isn’t that particular vowel in English (although it is voiced passingly in words like “new” or “dew”).

I’d pronounce the latter part of the name as “no-eh-la”. The “ë” means that the “e” should be pronounced separately as an “eh”; if it was written as “Noellat”, the “e” would get integrated as a part of the first syllable and then it would be pronounced as “no-la”.

So the whole name would be “Ü-de-loh NO-eh-la”.

John Morris:

If you’ll accept Austrian terms… I learned over Christmas the proper pronunciation of Grüner Veltliner. Since an initial V in German words is normally pronounced as F. Thus “viel” (many) = feel; vor (ahead) = for. So I’d assumed that the second word was pronounced FELT-li-ner, with a stress on the first syllable.

(I won’t attempt to explain the umlauted U in Grüner.)

And don’t forget the rhotic "r"s - no rounded English "r"s, everyone should try to sound as Scottish as possible when attempting to speak German! At least you get the Rs right.

The V as a “v” (instead of “f”) might be an Austrian thing, because I’ve heard many Germans say it as “felt-LI-ner”. The “grüner” part might be more difficult, but I guess it would make sense if you thought of the first syllable as a very Scottish pronounciation if the word “grew” but cut short, or said as quickly as possible, followed by “-ner”.

P Intag:

Um, no. It is pronounced OWS-leh-zeh. No “sh” sound.

This is pretty accurate, but from what I’ve heard, many Germans still, for some reason, make the first S almost “sh”, so occasionally you’ll hear it with a hushed “s” as “OWSh-leh-zeh”. But usually you’ll hear it as that “OWS-leh-zeh”.

I could explain how that is pronounced as well, but perhaps you are more interested in Pouilly-Fuissé?

The double-L here is pronounced as /j/, i.e. as in tortilla (torti_/j/_a or just “tortiya”), making the end of the first part sound something like “eww”:

PWA-eww füi-seh

The “fui-” part in Fuissé is a bit hard to describe accurately, unless you understand how “ü” is pronounced. It is something in the vein of “fyi-”, “fwi-” or “fui”.

I’m sorry to be blunt, but you seem to be totally confused with the terminology and orthography.

Merriam Websters uses unconventionally “ā” to represent the diphthong /ei/, aka. the vowel in “face”. Normally in dictionaries ā is used to represent long /a/ (think of “mud” but with a long “u” instead of the short one). This is why it might seem weird. However, I’m very certain nobody pronounces “say” with “s” and then the long vowel from “mud”.

Here’s a little source because you might ask for one: “It (ā) is used in some orthography-based transcriptions of English to represent the diphthong /eɪ/”

en.wikipedia.org

Ā

Ā, lowercase ā ("A with macron"), is a grapheme, a Latin A with a macron, used in several orthographies. Ā is used to denote a long A. Examples are the Baltic languages (e.g. Latvian), Polynesian languages, including Māori, some romanizations of Japanese, Persian, Pashto, Assyrian Neo-Aramaic (which represents a long A sound) and Arabic, and some Latin texts (especially for learners). In Romanised Mandarin Chinese (pinyin) it is used to represent A spoken with a level high tone (first tone). It i...

Then you proceed to explain how OED describes it as /sei/ and says how the /ei/ sound is pronounced as is the “a” in “face”. Yet you claim there’s no diphthong. I’m not sure if you are trolling or completely oblivious, but you are contradicting yourself. /ei/ is a diphthong composed of /e/ and /i/. In English language “a” on its own is normally pronounced as a diphthong /ei/ - “face” is a perfect example of this. If you pronounced with a vowel /a/, you’d pronounce it the same as you would pronounce “fus”. If you pronounced it with the vowel /e/, you’d pronounce it the same as “fes”. And so on.

The problem with the site you are watching, which instructs on American pronunciation, is that it is trying to make you produce the flatter long a in American speech. For an American, that sound remains identical to the a in face and will be how he or she produces the é sound in French unless and until they learn better. You can, of course, make phonetic distinctions between American and British vowels, but for noting pronunciation on this web, you will only lead people astray, as in, for instance, saying that an é should be pronounced as in meh.

As a university-trained linguist who has studied his fair share of English and French, I’m trying my best to explain people terms and things while not underestimating my readers here, but perhaps I’m failing here because of that. I guess the biggest pitfall here is that most of you people speak mainly or only English with very little or no idea of phonetic notation, so many of you seem to misread my attempts at pronunciation help (probably all the other internet pronunciation guides as well).

However, with your example, I still think that my original example “meh” to represent the quality of “é” is still sound. It is not a diphthong /ei/ as “a” in “face”, but instead one vowel, /e/, that you use in “meh”, “rest” or “pet”. You just have to pronounced it long, like “reeeest”. It is the same, no matter if it is American or British English we are talking about (unless we are talking about any rural dialects where the pronunciation just goes haywire).

Yes!

Listening to the English version of French is quite cringey because the ending “e” in French is not a diphthong, but English-speaking people naturally pronounce them as diphthongs, making it sound quite humoristic to a trained ear.

For example pâté (a French loanword) is normally pronounced more like “puh-tey” (with a final diphthong) in English, whereas the original French word is pronounced as “puh-teh” (with a bit length added to the latter vowel) without any diphthong. This same thing applies to anything ending with an -é in the French language.

The first step in removing the English influence in your French pronunciation is to rid yourselves of the tendency to make all vowels into diphthongs!

posts.name: Otto Forsberg

Jonathan Loesberg:

If to your ear, there is no significant difference on those youtube pronunciation sites between the é sound as it is pronounced there and let or fett in English, which is, effectively, to say that there is no long A sound in French, then all I can say is that everybody else needs to be aware of this aspect of your hearing when they follow your transcriptions of French pronunciation.

As a university-trained linguist who has been taught French since the age of 9, I’m starting to get slightly irked by your hardheadedness.

So the first thing I did was some googling to get us on the same page. I have been constantly thrown off by this “long a” you keep talking about, as you seem to use it in a wildly different manner compared to anything else I’ve learned (remember I’m a linguist). And apparently yes, there is a difference in meaning:

en.wikipedia.org

Vowel length

In linguistics, vowel length is the perceived length of a vowel sound: the corresponding physical measurement is duration. In some languages vowel length is an important phonemic factor, meaning vowel length can change the meaning of the word, for example in Arabic, Estonian, Finnish, Fijian, Japanese, Kannada, Kyrgyz, Latin, Malayalam, Old English, Scottish Gaelic, Tamil and Vietnamese. While vowel length alone does not change word meaning in most dialects of modern English, it is said to do so...

English vowels are sometimes split into “long” and “short” vowels along lines different from the linguistic differentiation. Traditionally, the vowels /eɪ iː aɪ oʊ juː/ (as in bait beat bite boat bute) are said to be the “long” counterparts of the vowels /æ ɛ ɪ ɒ ʌ/ (as in bat bet bit bot but) which are said to be “short”. (…) However, the use of the terms “long” and “short” in describing these vowel values is actually linguistically incorrect, as the letters in these words, although the same letter may be used, actually represent different vowels, that is, they are pronounced in spoken English as different vowels.

(…)

However, it is important to remember that the “a” in the word “mat” represents a different vowel sound than the “a” in “mate,” it is not a long or short version of the same vowel sound. In this case, the terms “long” and “short” are incorrectly used to describe these vowel values.

So these things are unlinguistic terms taught to you probably at the elementary school level, but - as stated above - the use of the term is incorrect. Most likely they are something that everyone who has been schooled in the English-speaking world know, but that term reflects only English pronunciation (and the term originates The Great Vowel Shift, reflecting the pronunciation back then, not now, centuries later). Using them is incorrect but understandable in English context, but using them in the context of other languages just doesn’t make sense.

So, I can understand why you might be confused by my explanations, me saying that there is no diphthong (i.e. what you seem to refer to as “long a”, for example) in the French final -é, but it is instead pronounced long (which you seem to understand as a diphthong, from the aforementioned “long a”). So to an non-linguistically trained eye that might seem like I am contradicting myself. Understandable. However, as I already said long time ago, I’m not trying to underestimate my readers here but instead trying to be as precise as possible with the limitations of not resorting to use phonetic alphabet, because to my best understanding, most of the people here don’t have a clue how to read them.

Now then, I really, really want to know what is the difference between the é sound and for example the “e” in “fett” and how would you describe it. And what is the aspect of pronunciation where I am wrong? The length of the vowel, its openness, diphthongization, wholly different vowel, what? I’m genuinely both interested and very confused.

For reference, let us take one of your videos, the word “marché” here (which perfectly shows how the ending -é is pronounced in French): https://youtu.be/10YcHUMHTJE?t=51

To me, this is like a Master of Wine trying to explain to somebody that Vintage Port is not red wine but instead fortified wine and then that somebody was laughing at me saying, “You really don’t know anything about wine, anyone can plainly see that Vintage Port is red and we were taught back home that all wines that are red are called “red wine”. Oh and I don’t know anything about Vintage Ports or fortified wines!”

As a side note, you seem not to have gotten the entire tone of Neal’s irony. Perhaps it was the accent aigu over it that threw you off.

I thought he was just commenting on your issues with the French pronunciation. Of course my sarcasm radar might have been a bit off.

The argument arose whether it is a diphthong /ie/ (or a “long a”, as you call it) or not (and it most certainly isn’t), and that was the major beef in pronouncing the words more the “French” way and not “americanized” with diphthongs (i.e. this “long a” in “face”).

And to clarify things a bit, here’s “fête” pronounced in French:

and here’s “marché” pronounced in French:

The first one is more open /ɛ/ and the second one is a bit closed /e/, so there is a subtle difference in the openness, but both of them are most certainly the vowel “e” (NB: not pronounced as you would in English, but as you would pronounce “e” in “pet”) and not a diphthong. Is that what you Americans are taught? Wow. That explains why practically everybody (including those who speak French surprisingly fluently) speak with a very americanized twang - a majority of which comes from the tendency to diphthongize vowels. And unlike your claim some posts ago, “a” in face is a diphthong /ei/. Unless you’re a Scot, because in many dialects of Scottish English it is actually a long vowel /ee/ - which is the same as -é in French. So yes, I do think that the standard method of teaching French pronunciation is manifestly wrong. No matter how long it has been taught, it is very wrong. None of the French teachers I’ve had has ever taught me an é (or any regular e in French) is a diphthong nor have I ever heard a French person using diphthongized /e/.

And while you were at it, I am not arguing all pedagogy is wrong. After all, I’ve done my pedagogical teacher studies at the university - that’s a hefty piece of 12 months - and I have language teacher’s papers. posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
Gerhard P.:
Otto Forsberg:
Gerhard P.:

What about: low-are … does that work?

" Low" sounds very wrong here to my ears. I’d say “loo-are” (with the “oo” cut short) or just “lu-are”. And yes, with the French throat R.

In “Loire” there is definitely no “u” …

Law-are ?

(I know exactly how to pronounce … but how to write it for an English tongue …?)

Jonathan’s “lwahr” actually might be the closest approximation. There’s no actual “u” there, but the brief vowel there between those “l” and “w” is definitely closer to “u” in “lu” than “aw” in “law”.

And yes, there’s an “s” pronounced at the end of Vacqueyras. I’d love to know what is the logic behind the silent / non-silent s in French!

(Cahors, Cassis, Chénas, Cornas, Graves, Gigondas, Juliénas, Minervois, Riceys, Rivesaltes, Sauternes, Vacqueyras - I know how these are pronounced, but there seems to be no logic!)

And yes, there’s an “s” pronounced at the end of Vacqueyras. I’d love to know what is the logic behind the silent / non-silent s in French!

(Cahors, Cassis, Chénas, Cornas, Graves, Gigondas, Juliénas, Minervois, Riceys, Rivesaltes, Sauternes, Vacqueyras - I know how these are pronounced, but there seems to be no logic!)

To me there seems to be no logic either, but all are individual “names” … and we have to pronounce them as the owner/inhabitants do themselves.

BTW: Cassis - I once asked the owner of a Vinotheque in Cassis (town in Southern France) - and she told me that the town is pronounced without “s”-ending, while the Cassis Liqueur from blackcurrents is pronounced WITH “s”-ending.

BTW-2: Château Rayas: the “s” is pronounced !
